title: Minimum size subarray sum
description: Find smallest subarray length with sum at least target

examples.explanation: n=6, min_len=inf. i=0: current_sum=0. j=0: current_sum=2 (<7). j=1: current_sum=5 (<7). j=2: current_sum=6 (<7). j=3: current_sum=8 (≥7), length=4, min_len=4, break. i=1: current_sum=0. j=1: current_sum=3 (<7). j=2: current_sum=4 (<7). j=3: current_sum=6 (<7). j=4: current_sum=10 (≥7), length=4, min_len=4, break. i=2: current_sum=0. j=2: current_sum=1 (<7). j=3: current_sum=3 (<7). j=4: current_sum=7 (≥7), length=3, min_len=3, break. i=3: current_sum=0. j=3: current_sum=2 (<7). j=4: current_sum=6 (<7). j=5: current_sum=9 (≥7), length=3, min_len=3, break. i=4: current_sum=0. j=4: current_sum=4 (<7). j=5: current_sum=7 (≥7), length=2, min_len=2, break. i=5: current_sum=0. j=5: current_sum=3 (<7). Loop ends. min_len=2, return 2.

n=3, min_len=inf. i=0: current_sum=0. j=0: current_sum=1 (<4). j=1: current_sum=5 (≥4), length=2, min_len=2, break. i=1: current_sum=0. j=1: current_sum=4 (≥4), length=1, min_len=1, break. i=2: current_sum=0. j=2: current_sum=4 (≥4), length=1, min_len=1, break. min_len=1, return 1.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Check all possible subarrays by iterating through each starting index and expanding to find the minimum length where sum ≥ target. Use nested loops to compute sums for all subarray combinations.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Size Subarray Sum with Brute Force O(n²)
ladders.desc: Find the smallest subarray length where sum ≥ target by checking all possible subarrays using nested loops.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach systematically examines every possible contiguous subarray to find the minimum length meeting the sum condition. Intuitively, we need to consider all starting positions (i) and for each, expand to all possible ending positions (j ≥ i) to compute subarray sums. This exhaustive search guarantees we don't miss any candidate subarray. The algorithm uses two nested loops: outer loop iterates through each starting index i from 0 to n-1, inner loop iterates through each ending index j from i to n-1. For each (i,j) pair, we compute the sum of elements from i to j (inclusive). If this sum ≥ target, we update the minimum length found so far. We initialize min_len to infinity (or n+1) and return 0 if no valid subarray exists. Time complexity is O(n²) due to nested loops where each computes sums from scratch (though we could optimize with prefix sums, the basic brute force recomputes). Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. Connection to L1: This full solution relies on the key subroutine of computing subarray sums for all (i,j) pairs. Common mistakes include: 1) Not handling empty array or target=0 correctly (target=0 should return 1 for non-empty array since any single element satisfies sum≥0), 2) Forgetting to reset sum for each starting index, 3) Using ≤ instead of ≥ when comparing to target, 4) Not initializing min_len properly. Real code pattern: def minSubArrayLen(target, nums): n = len(nums); min_len = float('inf'); for i in range(n): current_sum = 0; for j in range(i, n): current_sum += nums[j]; if current_sum >= target: min_len = min(min_len, j-i+1); break; return 0 if min_len == float('inf') else min_len.

examples.trace: n=6, min_len=inf. i=0: current_sum=0. j=0: current_sum=2 (<7). j=1: current_sum=5 (<7). j=2: current_sum=6 (<7). j=3: current_sum=8 (≥7), length=4, min_len=4, break. i=1: current_sum=0. j=1: current_sum=3 (<7). j=2: current_sum=4 (<7). j=3: current_sum=6 (<7). j=4: current_sum=10 (≥7), length=4, min_len=4, break. i=2: current_sum=0. j=2: current_sum=1 (<7). j=3: current_sum=3 (<7). j=4: current_sum=7 (≥7), length=3, min_len=3, break. i=3: current_sum=0. j=3: current_sum=2 (<7). j=4: current_sum=6 (<7). j=5: current_sum=9 (≥7), length=3, min_len=3, break. i=4: current_sum=0. j=4: current_sum=4 (<7). j=5: current_sum=7 (≥7), length=2, min_len=2, break. i=5: current_sum=0. j=5: current_sum=3 (<7). Loop ends. min_len=2, return 2.

n=3, min_len=inf. i=0: current_sum=0. j=0: current_sum=1 (<4). j=1: current_sum=5 (≥4), length=2, min_len=2, break. i=1: current_sum=0. j=1: current_sum=4 (≥4), length=1, min_len=1, break. i=2: current_sum=0. j=2: current_sum=4 (≥4), length=1, min_len=1, break. min_len=1, return 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute All Subarray Sums for a Given Starting Index
ladders.desc: For a fixed starting index i, compute sums of all subarrays starting at i and find the first where sum ≥ target.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the brute force approach. Given a starting index i, we need to examine all possible ending indices j (from i to n-1) to find the shortest subarray from i that meets the sum condition. Intuitively, by fixing i and expanding j rightward, we can compute cumulative sums efficiently: start with sum=0, then for each j, add nums[j] to the sum. Once sum ≥ target, we've found a candidate subarray of length (j-i+1). We can break early since further expanding j would only increase length. The algorithm: Initialize current_sum = 0. For j from i to n-1: current_sum += nums[j]; if current_sum ≥ target: return length = j-i+1. If loop completes without meeting target, return infinity (or a sentinel). Time complexity for this subroutine is O(n) in worst case (when i=0 and no valid subarray exists). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each starting index i in the outer loop of the full solution. Common mistakes: 1) Not breaking early when sum ≥ target (continuing would find longer subarrays unnecessarily), 2) Forgetting to reset current_sum for each new i, 3) Incorrect length calculation (should be j-i+1, not j-i). Real code pattern: def find_min_len_from_start(i, target, nums): n = len(nums); current_sum = 0; for j in range(i, n): current_sum += nums[j]; if current_sum >= target: return j - i + 1; return float('inf').

examples.trace: n=6, current_sum=0. j=0: current_sum=2 (<7). j=1: current_sum=5 (<7). j=2: current_sum=6 (<7). j=3: current_sum=8 (≥7), length=4, return 4.
n=6, current_sum=0. j=4: current_sum=4 (<7). j=5: current_sum=7 (≥7), length=2, return 2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate Through All Possible Subarray Start and End Indices
ladders.desc: Generate all pairs (i,j) where i ≤ j to represent every contiguous subarray of an array.
ladders.explanation: The central technique in brute force is to systematically enumerate all contiguous subarrays. A subarray is defined by its starting index i and ending index j where 0 ≤ i ≤ j < n (n = array length). Intuitively, we need to consider every possible i as a starting point, and for each i, every possible j ≥ i as an ending point. This ensures we cover all subarrays without omission. The algorithm uses nested loops: outer loop for i from 0 to n-1, inner loop for j from i to n-1. For each (i,j) pair, we can perform operations (like computing sum). The total number of pairs is n*(n+1)/2, which is O(n²). This exhaustive enumeration is why brute force has quadratic time complexity. Connection to L1: This logic provides the framework for calling the key subroutine for each starting index i. Common mistakes: 1) Using j from 0 to n-1 instead of i to n-1 (which would include non-contiguous segments or double-count), 2) Not handling empty array (n=0) where no pairs exist, 3) Confusing subarray with subsequence (which doesn't require contiguity). Real code pattern: n = len(nums); for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): # process subarray nums[i:j+1].

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Sum of a Contiguous Subarray Given Start and End Indices
ladders.desc: Calculate the total sum of elements from index i to j (inclusive) in an array.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for evaluating subarrays. Given an array nums and indices i and j (with i ≤ j), the sum of the subarray nums[i:j+1] is the addition of all elements at positions i, i+1, ..., j. Intuitively, we traverse the segment and accumulate values. The algorithm: Initialize sum = 0. For index k from i to j (inclusive): sum += nums[k]. Return sum. Time complexity is O(length) where length = j-i+1, which can be up to O(n). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L2: This operation is performed for each (i,j) pair generated by the core logic. In the brute force approach, we often compute this sum from scratch each time, leading to O(n³) if done naively, but in optimized brute force, we use cumulative sums within loops to achieve O(n²). Common mistakes: 1) Off-by-one errors (using j exclusive instead of inclusive), 2) Not validating i and j are within array bounds, 3) Forgetting to initialize sum to 0. Real code pattern: def subarray_sum(nums, i, j): total = 0; for k in range(i, j+1): total += nums[k]; return total.

examples.trace: sum=0. k=1: sum=3. k=2: sum=4. k=3: sum=6. Return 6.
sum=0. k=0: sum=5. k=1: sum=15. k=2: sum=30. Return 30.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Through Array Indices with a Loop
ladders.desc: Use a loop to access each element of an array sequentially by its index.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves traversing an array from start to end using an index variable. Intuitively, arrays are stored in contiguous memory, and we can access elements by their position (index). A loop (like for or while) allows us to visit each index in order. The algorithm: Initialize index i = 0. While i < length of array: access array[i]; increment i by 1. In pseudocode, this is often written as for i in range(len(array)): process array[i]. Time complexity is O(n) for traversing n elements. Space complexity is O(1) if no extra data structures are used. Connection to L3: This operation is used within the subarray sum computation to iterate from i to j. Common mistakes: 1) Using i ≤ length instead of i < length (causes index out of bounds), 2) Modifying the array while iterating (can lead to unexpected behavior), 3) Confusing zero-based indexing (common in programming) with one-based indexing. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(arr)): val = arr[i].

examples.trace: len=3. i=0: access arr[0]=10. i=1: access arr[1]=20. i=2: access arr[2]=30. i=3: loop ends.
len=1. i=0: access arr[0]=5. i=1: loop ends.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Learn what an array is and how to access elements using indices.
ladders.explanation: An array is a fundamental data structure that stores a collection of elements in contiguous memory locations. Each element has a position called an index, typically starting from 0 (zero-based indexing). Intuitively, think of an array as a row of boxes numbered 0,1,2,...; each box holds a value. To access an element, you specify its index: array[index] returns the value at that position. Key concepts: 1) Arrays have a fixed size (length) when created. 2) Indices must be within range [0, length-1] to avoid errors. 3) Elements can be of any data type (e.g., integers). This foundation is crucial because the minimum subarray sum problem operates on arrays. Connection to L4: This knowledge enables iterating through arrays using loops. Common mistakes: 1) Assuming indices start at 1, 2) Accessing index equal to length (which is out of bounds), 3) Not understanding that arrays are mutable (values can be changed). Real code pattern: In Python, arr = [3,1,4]; first_element = arr[0] (value 3); second_element = arr[1] (value 1).

examples.trace: Array has indices: 0→5, 1→10, 2→15. Access arr[1] returns value 10.
Array has indices: 0→2, 1→4, 2→6, 3→8. Access arr[0] returns value 2.

approaches.name: Binary Search on Prefix Sum O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Compute prefix sums, then for each starting index, use binary search to find the earliest ending index where subarray sum ≥ target. This reduces search time per start index from O(n) to O(log n).

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Size Subarray Sum with Binary Search on Prefix Sum
ladders.desc: Complete solution using prefix sums and binary search to find smallest subarray length with sum ≥ target.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the minimum size subarray sum problem efficiently in O(n log n) time. The intuition is that by computing prefix sums, we can transform the problem of finding subarray sums into finding differences between prefix sums. For each starting index i, we need to find the smallest ending index j such that prefix[j] - prefix[i] ≥ target, which means subarray sum from i to j-1 is ≥ target. Instead of scanning linearly for each i (which would be O(n²)), we use binary search on the prefix array to find j in O(log n) time per i. The algorithm: 1) Compute prefix sums array where prefix[0]=0 and prefix[i]=sum of first i elements. 2) Initialize answer as infinity. 3) For each starting index i from 0 to n-1: a) Calculate target_prefix = prefix[i] + target. b) Use binary search to find the smallest index j where prefix[j] ≥ target_prefix. c) If found, update answer with min(answer, j-i). 4) Return answer if found, else 0. Time complexity is O(n log n) for n binary searches, space is O(n) for prefix array. Connection to L1: This uses the binary search helper from L1 repeatedly. Common mistakes: forgetting prefix[0]=0, off-by-one errors in indices, not handling case where no subarray exists. Real code pattern: def minSubArrayLen(target, nums): n=len(nums); prefix=[0]*(n+1); for i in range(n): prefix[i+1]=prefix[i]+nums[i]; ans=float('inf'); for i in range(n): to_find=prefix[i]+target; j=binary_search(prefix, to_find); if j<=n: ans=min(ans, j-i); return 0 if ans==float('inf') else ans.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute prefix sums: prefix=[0,2,5,6,8,12,15]. Step 2: Initialize ans=inf. Step 3: For i=0: prefix[0]=0, to_find=0+7=7, binary search in prefix for ≥7 finds j=4 (prefix[4]=8), subarray length=4-0=4, ans=4. i=1: prefix[1]=2, to_find=9, binary search finds j=5 (prefix[5]=12), length=5-1=4, ans=4. i=2: prefix[2]=5, to_find=12, binary search finds j=5 (prefix[5]=12), length=5-2=3, ans=3. i=3: prefix[3]=6, to_find=13, binary search finds j=6 (prefix[6]=15), length=6-3=3, ans=3. i=4: prefix[4]=8, to_find=15, binary search finds j=6 (prefix[6]=15), length=6-4=2, ans=2. i=5: prefix[5]=12, to_find=19, binary search finds no j (j=7>n), skip. Step 4: Return ans=2.

Step 1: prefix=[0,1,3,6,10,15]. Step 2: ans=inf. Step 3: i=0: to_find=11, binary search finds j=5 (prefix[5]=15), length=5, ans=5. i=1: to_find=12, binary search finds j=5 (prefix[5]=15), length=4, ans=4. i=2: to_find=13, binary search finds j=5 (prefix[5]=15), length=3, ans=3. i=3: to_find=14, binary search finds j=5 (prefix[5]=15), length=2, ans=3 (no update). i=4: to_find=15, binary search finds j=5 (prefix[5]=15), length=1, ans=3 (no update). Step 4: Return ans=3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Binary Search to Find First Prefix Sum ≥ Target
ladders.desc: Helper function that finds the smallest index in a sorted prefix array where value ≥ target.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core efficiency booster in the main algorithm. The intuition is that prefix sums are non-decreasing (since array elements are non-negative in typical problem constraints, but algorithm works generally), allowing binary search. Given a sorted array prefix and a target value to_find, we need the smallest index j such that prefix[j] ≥ to_find. This is a standard 'lower_bound' or 'bisect_left' operation. Algorithm: 1) Initialize left=0, right=len(prefix)-1, answer=len(prefix) (default if not found). 2) While left ≤ right: a) mid = (left+right)//2. b) If prefix[mid] ≥ to_find: update answer=mid, right=mid-1 (search left for smaller index). c) Else: left=mid+1. 3) Return answer. Time O(log n), space O(1). Connection to L0: This is called for each starting index i to find ending index j. Connection to L2: Implements binary search logic on sorted data. Common mistakes: infinite loops from incorrect bounds, off-by-one in array length, not handling not-found case. Real code pattern: def binary_search(prefix, target): left,right=0,len(prefix)-1; ans=len(prefix); while left<=right: mid=(left+right)//2; if prefix[mid]>=target: ans=mid; right=mid-1; else: left=mid+1; return ans.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=6, ans=7. Step 2: mid=(0+6)//2=3, prefix[3]=6 <7, so left=4. Step 3: left=4, right=6, mid=5, prefix[5]=12 ≥7, ans=5, right=4. Step 4: left=4, right=4, mid=4, prefix[4]=8 ≥7, ans=4, right=3. Step 5: left=4, right=3, loop ends. Return ans=4.

Step 1: left=0, right=5, ans=6. Step 2: mid=2, prefix[2]=6 <13, left=3. Step 3: left=3, right=5, mid=4, prefix[4]=10 <13, left=5. Step 4: left=5, right=5, mid=5, prefix[5]=15 ≥13, ans=5, right=4. Step 5: left=5, right=4, loop ends. Return ans=5.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Using Prefix Sums to Express Subarray Sums
ladders.desc: Technique to compute subarray sums efficiently via prefix sum differences.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight that enables efficient searching. The intuition is that any subarray sum from index i to j-1 (0-indexed) can be computed as prefix[j] - prefix[i], where prefix[k] is the sum of first k elements (with prefix[0]=0). This transforms the problem from O(n) per subarray to O(1) per query once prefix is built. Why it works: By definition, prefix[j] = sum of nums[0..j-1] and prefix[i] = sum of nums[0..i-1], so their difference gives sum of nums[i..j-1]. This allows us to precompute prefix sums in O(n) time and then query any subarray sum instantly. In the main problem, for each i, we need j such that prefix[j] - prefix[i] ≥ target, which rearranges to prefix[j] ≥ prefix[i] + target. Since prefix is sorted (if nums are non-negative), we can binary search for j. Algorithm: 1) Build prefix array of length n+1: prefix[0]=0; for i from 1 to n: prefix[i]=prefix[i-1]+nums[i-1]. 2) Now sum(nums[i..j-1]) = prefix[j]-prefix[i]. Time O(n) to build, space O(n). Connection to L0: This provides the array for binary search. Connection to L3: Builds on cumulative sum concept. Common mistakes: wrong prefix length (should be n+1), incorrect indexing leading to off-by-one errors. Real code pattern: def build_prefix(nums): n=len(nums); prefix=[0]*(n+1); for i in range(n): prefix[i+1]=prefix[i]+nums[i]; return prefix.

ladders.title: Building Block — Cumulative Sum (Prefix Sum) Concept
ladders.desc: Intermediate concept of computing running totals of an array.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental technique in algorithm design where we precompute cumulative sums to answer range sum queries quickly. The intuition is to avoid recomputing sums repeatedly by storing intermediate results. For an array nums of length n, the prefix sum array prefix has length n+1 with prefix[0]=0 and prefix[i] = sum of nums[0..i-1] for i≥1. This means prefix[i] represents the total sum from the start up to index i-1. Why it's useful: Once built in O(n) time, any subarray sum from index a to b can be found in O(1) as prefix[b+1] - prefix[a]. This is efficient compared to summing elements each time (O(b-a+1)). Algorithm to build: 1) Initialize prefix[0]=0. 2) For i from 1 to n: prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] + nums[i-1]. Time O(n), space O(n). Connection to L2: This is the implementation of prefix sums. Connection to L4: Uses addition operation repeatedly. Common mistakes: indexing errors (e.g., using nums[i] instead of nums[i-1]), forgetting prefix[0]=0. Real code pattern: def cumulative_sum(nums): prefix=[0]; for num in nums: prefix.append(prefix[-1]+num); return prefix.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start prefix=[0]. Step 2: Add nums[0]=3: prefix=[0,3]. Step 3: Add nums[1]=1: prefix=[0,3,4]. Step 4: Add nums[2]=4: prefix=[0,3,4,8]. Step 5: Add nums[3]=2: prefix=[0,3,4,8,10]. Result: [0,3,4,8,10].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterative Summation of Array Elements
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of adding numbers sequentially in a loop.
ladders.explanation: This is a simple yet essential operation where we compute the total sum of elements in an array by iterating through each element and accumulating the result. The intuition is to process elements one by one, maintaining a running total. For an array nums, we initialize sum=0, then for each element num in nums, we update sum += num. This yields the total sum in O(n) time and O(1) space. Why it's foundational: Many algorithms, including prefix sums, rely on this basic iteration and addition. It teaches control flow (loops) and variable updating. Algorithm: 1) Initialize total=0. 2) For each element x in array: total = total + x. 3) Return total. Time O(n), space O(1). Connection to L3: This is repeated in cumulative sum building for each element. Connection to L5: Uses addition operation. Common mistakes: not initializing sum to 0, off-by-one in loop bounds, using wrong variable. Real code pattern: def sum_array(arr): total=0; for x in arr: total+=x; return total.

examples.trace: Step 1: total=0. Step 2: Process 2: total=0+2=2. Step 3: Process 3: total=2+3=5. Step 4: Process 1: total=5+1=6. Step 5: Process 2: total=6+2=8. Step 6: Process 4: total=8+4=12. Step 7: Process 3: total=12+3=15. Result: 15.
Step 1: total=0. Step 2: Process 1: total=1. Step 3: Process 2: total=3. Step 4: Process 3: total=6. Step 5: Process 4: total=10. Step 6: Process 5: total=15. Result: 15.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Addition of Two Numbers
ladders.explanation: This is the most fundamental concept required: adding two numbers together. The intuition is combining quantities to get a total. For numbers a and b, the sum is a + b. In programming, this is done using the + operator. Why it's foundational: All summation operations in algorithms build on this basic arithmetic. It's essential for accumulating values in loops, computing prefix sums, and more. Example: 2 + 3 = 5. In code, result = a + b. Time O(1), space O(1). Connection to L4: Repeated addition in a loop sums array elements. Common mistakes: overflow with large numbers (but not in basic learning), misunderstanding operator precedence. Real code pattern: def add(a,b): return a+b.

examples.trace: Step 1: Take numbers 2 and 3. Step 2: Add them: 2 + 3 = 5. Result: 5.
Step 1: Numbers 10 and -5. Step 2: Add: 10 + (-5) = 5. Result: 5.

approaches.name: Sliding Window Two Pointer O(n)
approaches.summary: Maintain a dynamic window using two pointers (left and right) that expands to include elements until sum ≥ target, then contracts from the left to find the minimum length while maintaining the condition.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Size Subarray Sum with Sliding Window
ladders.desc: Complete solution finding smallest subarray length with sum ≥ target using two-pointer sliding window.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the smallest contiguous subarray whose sum is at least a given target value. The sliding window approach provides an optimal O(n) solution by dynamically adjusting a window defined by two pointers (left and right). The intuition is that we can expand the window by moving the right pointer to include more elements until the sum meets or exceeds the target. Once this condition is satisfied, we contract the window from the left by moving the left pointer forward to find the minimum possible length while still maintaining sum ≥ target. This works because when we have a valid window, any smaller window must be found by removing elements from the left side—we never need to backtrack the right pointer. The algorithm maintains a running sum and tracks the minimum length found. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize left = 0, min_len = infinity, current_sum = 0. 2. Iterate right from 0 to n-1: add nums[right] to current_sum. 3. While current_sum ≥ target: update min_len with (right-left+1), subtract nums[left] from current_sum, increment left. 4. After loop, return min_len if updated else 0. Time complexity is O(n) since each element is processed at most twice (once by right pointer, once by left). Space is O(1). Connection to L1: This directly implements the sliding window subroutine. Common mistakes include not handling empty result (return 0), using nested loops incorrectly (should be while not if), or initializing min_len incorrectly. Real code pattern: def minSubArrayLen(target, nums): left = min_len = 0; current_sum = 0; for right in range(len(nums)): current_sum += nums[right]; while current_sum >= target: min_len = min(min_len, right-left+1) if min_len else right-left+1; current_sum -= nums[left]; left += 1; return min_len.

examples.trace: Initialize: left=0, min_len=inf, sum=0. Right=0: sum=2 (<7). Right=1: sum=5 (<7). Right=2: sum=6 (<7). Right=3: sum=8 (≥7): update min_len=4 (window [2,3,1,2]), subtract nums[0]=2, sum=6, left=1. While loop ends (6<7). Right=4: sum=10 (≥7): update min_len=4 (window [3,1,2,4]), subtract nums[1]=3, sum=7, left=2, still ≥7: update min_len=3 (window [1,2,4]), subtract nums[2]=1, sum=6, left=3. While ends. Right=5: sum=9 (≥7): update min_len=3 (window [2,4,3]), subtract nums[3]=2, sum=7, left=4, still ≥7: update min_len=2 (window [4,3]), subtract nums[4]=4, sum=3, left=5. While ends. Return min_len=2.

Initialize: left=0, min_len=inf, sum=0. Right=0: sum=1 (<11). Right=1: sum=3 (<11). Right=2: sum=6 (<11). Right=3: sum=10 (<11). Right=4: sum=15 (≥11): update min_len=5 (window [1,2,3,4,5]), subtract nums[0]=1, sum=14, left=1, still ≥11: update min_len=4 (window [2,3,4,5]), subtract nums[1]=2, sum=12, left=2, still ≥11: update min_len=3 (window [3,4,5]), subtract nums[2]=3, sum=9, left=3. While ends. Return min_len=3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Sliding Window Sum Checker
ladders.desc: Implement a function that finds if any subarray sum meets target using expanding/contracting window.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core engine of the full solution: given an array and target, it uses two pointers to maintain a dynamic window, expanding by moving the right pointer to include elements until the sum reaches or exceeds the target, then contracting from the left to find the minimal valid window. The intuition is that by adjusting the window boundaries incrementally, we can efficiently explore all potential subarrays without redundant calculations. This works because once we have a window with sum ≥ target, any smaller valid window must be a contraction of it from the left—we never need to consider windows that start earlier, as they would be larger. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize left = 0, current_sum = 0, found = False. 2. For right from 0 to n-1: add nums[right] to current_sum. 3. While current_sum ≥ target: set found = True (or track min length), subtract nums[left] from current_sum, increment left. 4. Return found or min length. Time O(n), space O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine is directly embedded in the full problem to compute the minimum length. Common mistakes: not using a while loop for contraction (using if instead), or incorrectly updating pointers. Real code pattern: def sliding_window_sum(nums, target): left = sum_val = 0; for right in range(len(nums)): sum_val += nums[right]; while sum_val >= target: # Process valid window; sum_val -= nums[left]; left += 1; return result.

examples.trace: Initialize: left=0, sum=0. Right=0: sum=1 (<6). Right=1: sum=3 (<6). Right=2: sum=6 (≥6): found=True, subtract nums[0]=1, sum=5, left=1. While ends (5<6). Right=3: sum=9 (≥6): found=True, subtract nums[1]=2, sum=7, left=2, still ≥6: subtract nums[2]=3, sum=4, left=3. While ends. Return True.
Initialize: left=0, sum=0. Right=0: sum=1 (<10). Right=1: sum=3 (<10). Right=2: sum=6 (<10). End loop, sum never ≥10, return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Two-Pointer Window Adjustment
ladders.desc: Master the technique of moving two pointers to maintain a condition on subarray sum.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental technique of using two pointers (indices) to define a window in an array and adjusting them based on a condition—specifically, expanding the window to increase sum and contracting it to decrease sum. The intuition is that by controlling the window size dynamically, we can efficiently traverse the array in O(n) time without nested loops. This works because the pointers move monotonically forward: right pointer expands to include new elements, and left pointer contracts only when the condition is met, ensuring each element is processed a constant number of times. The key insight is that for problems like minimum subarray sum, once we find a valid window, we only need to try making it smaller by moving the left pointer; we never need to reconsider earlier right positions. Step-by-step: 1. Start with left=0, right=0, sum=0. 2. While right < n: add nums[right] to sum; if sum meets condition, move left forward while condition holds to find smaller window; increment right. 3. Track results during adjustments. Time O(n), space O(1). Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the sliding window subroutine. Common mistakes: moving pointers in wrong order, or not handling edge cases like empty arrays. Real code pattern: left = right = 0; sum = 0; while right < n: sum += nums[right]; while condition(sum): # Adjust left; sum -= nums[left]; left += 1; right += 1.

examples.trace: Initialize: left=0, right=0, sum=0. Right=0: sum=2 (<3). Right=1: sum=3 (≥3): condition met, window [2,1], move left: subtract nums[0]=2, sum=1, left=1. While ends. Right=2: sum=4 (≥3): window [1,3], move left: subtract nums[1]=1, sum=3, left=2, still ≥3: subtract nums[2]=3, sum=0, left=3. While ends. End.
Initialize: left=0, right=0, sum=0. Right=0: sum=1 (<4). Right=1: sum=2 (<4). Right=2: sum=3 (<4). End, sum never ≥4.

ladders.title: Building Block — Prefix Sum and Window Sum Calculation
ladders.desc: Learn to compute the sum of any subarray efficiently using cumulative sums or running totals.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves calculating the sum of a contiguous subarray defined by two pointers. The intuition is that by maintaining a running total or using prefix sums, we can compute subarray sums in O(1) time after preprocessing, avoiding O(n) recalculations. For the sliding window approach, we use a running sum: as we expand the window by moving the right pointer, we add the new element to the sum; as we contract by moving the left pointer, we subtract the removed element. This works because the sum of the window [left, right] is the cumulative sum up to right minus the sum up to left-1, but with running totals, we update incrementally. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize sum = 0. 2. To expand: sum += nums[right]. 3. To contract: sum -= nums[left]. 4. This keeps sum as the total of nums[left...right]. Time O(1) per operation, space O(1). Connection to L2: This enables efficient window adjustment in the two-pointer logic. Common mistakes: forgetting to update sum when pointers move, or off-by-one errors in indices. Real code pattern: sum = 0; for right in range(n): sum += nums[right]; # Expand; while condition: sum -= nums[left]; left += 1 # Contract.

examples.trace: Start sum=0. Expand to right=0: sum=0+2=2. Expand to right=1: sum=2+3=5. Expand to right=2: sum=5+1=6. Contract left=0: sum=6-2=4, left=1. Contract left=1: sum=4-3=1, left=2. Final sum for window [2,2] is 1.

Start sum=0. Suppose we have window from left=1 to right=3: sum initially for [0,3] would be 4, but with running: expand to right=0: sum=1; right=1: sum=2; right=2: sum=3; right=3: sum=4. Then contract left=0: sum=4-1=3, left=1. So window [1,3] sum=3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating with Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Practice moving two indices through an array independently and in coordination.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves using two integer variables (pointers) to track positions in an array, and updating them based on simple rules. The intuition is that by controlling two indices separately, we can represent ranges or windows within the array, which is essential for many algorithms. This works because arrays provide O(1) access by index, allowing efficient traversal. For sliding window, we typically move the right pointer to expand the window and the left pointer to contract it, ensuring they only move forward to maintain O(n) time. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize left = 0, right = 0. 2. While right < array length: perform operation with current window [left, right]; increment right to expand. 3. Under some condition, increment left to contract. 4. This processes each element a constant number of times. Time O(n), space O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used to adjust the window for sum calculations. Common mistakes: moving pointers in infinite loops, or not checking bounds. Real code pattern: left = 0; for right in range(n): # Process; while condition: left += 1.

examples.input: nums = [a,b,c], move right then left when right reaches index 1
examples.trace: Start left=0, right=0: window [a]. Right=1: window [a,b]. Suppose condition met: left=1, window [b]. Right=2: window [b,c]. End.
Left=0 fixed. Right=0: [x]. Right=1: [x,y]. Right=2: [x,y,z]. End.

testCases.input: nums = [1,2,3], start left=0, right moves to 2
nums = [p,q,r,s], right moves to 3, left moves at 2
nums = [1,1,1], left and right both start 0
nums = [3,7], right to 1, left to 0 then 1

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Traversal and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understand how to access and loop through array elements using indices.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: working with arrays (or lists) by using indices to access elements sequentially. An array is a collection of items stored in contiguous memory, accessible in O(1) time via an integer index starting from 0. The intuition is that by iterating through indices, we can process each element one by one, which is foundational for any array algorithm. This works because arrays provide direct access to any position using the base address and index offset. Step-by-step: 1. Define an array, e.g., nums = [a,b,c]. 2. Use a loop with an index variable i from 0 to length-1. 3. In each iteration, access nums[i] to read or modify the element. 4. This allows linear traversal. Time O(n) for n elements, space O(1). Connection to L4: This basic traversal enables moving pointers in the array. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (e.g., using <= instead of <), or accessing out-of-bounds indices. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(nums)): element = nums[i] # Process element.

examples.trace: i=0: nums[0]=10, print 10. i=1: nums[1]=20, print 20. i=2: nums[2]=30, print 30. End.
Index 2 refers to the third element: nums[2] = 4.
